I bring data discipline, emerging market knowledge, and a genuine interest in technology-driven financial transformation — the trifecta that drives fundable opportunities in the markets I understand best.
// Analytical Work
Projects
DigiCorp Ltd. — Full VC Deal Valuation (4 Methods)
VC Core · Deal Valuation
Individual Coursework · Private Equity Module · Cranfield University · April 2025

End-to-end valuation of DigiCorp Ltd., a high-growth early-stage UK technology company, using four methodologies as a VC analyst would present to an investment committee. The analysis modelled two performance scenarios with full debt structuring, tax shield capture, and ownership mechanics.

Key Assumptions
Discount rate (DCF)35% — reflects early-stage technology risk premium
Required return (VC)60% — standard VC hurdle for early-stage deals
Terminal growth rate5% — Gordon Growth Model; aligned to long-run GDP + inflation
Tax rate35% — standard corporate tax applied to EBIT
DepreciationStraight-line over 5 years on Net PPE; CapEx = change in Net Fixed Assets year-on-year
NWC definitionOperating cash + receivables + inventories + prepaid expenses, less payables and other liabilities. Cash excluded
ScenariosBase case: 100% projected cash flows. Downside: 50% cash flows and 50% NWC
Debt structure (CCF)£2M in 2025, £5M in 2026, £10M in 2027; interest rate 10%; tax shield = Debt × Rate × Tax
Cost of equity (CAPM)Rf 7% (10yr UK gilt), market premium 7%, unlevered beta 1.28 (avg of 4 comparables) → Ke = 15.96%
Beta derivationAll comparable firms are equity-financed, so levered beta equals unlevered beta; average of Sky PLC (1.45), OneTech (1.33), Glow (1.12), JCB (1.23)
Scenario weights (NPV)35% full cash flows · 35% downside · 30% failure (zero value)
Existing shares (VC)1,000,000 shares assumed at incorporation; P/E from 4 publicly listed comparables averaged at 24.24x
Relative Valuation — Comparable Companies (P/E)
CompanyP/E RatioLevered BetaD/E Ratio
Sky PLC19.51x1.450.59
OneTech PLC39.87x1.330.03
Glow PLC18.23x1.120.21
JCB PLC19.38x1.230.00
Average (applied to DigiCorp) 24.24x 1.28 0.21
Key Findings
VC Method: exit value of £326M (2027 net earnings × 24.24x P/E), discounted at 60% over 5 years to a present value of £31.1M; £1M investment secures 3.21% ownership at a share price of £30.11
Base case DCF: enterprise value of £115.9M; incorporating CCF debt tax shields pushes this to £127.8M, a 10.3% uplift from leverage — the financial benefit of a PE-backed buyout structure
Risk-adjusted Scenario-Weighted NPV of £68.6M (CCF basis) after assigning 30% probability to full failure — the IC-ready number that accounts for early-stage execution risk
CCF captures £466K in tax shields (present value) across three debt tranches, demonstrating the financing advantage not visible in a standard unlevered DCF
DCF Base: £115.9M
CCF Base: £127.8M
Scenario NPV: £68.6M
Exit Value: £326M
Ownership: 3.21%
Ke: 15.96%
Becton Dickinson / Thermo Fisher — Corporate Restructuring & M&A Deal Analysis
M&A · Market Intelligence
Group Project · Cranfield University MSc Finance · 2025

Analysed BD's proposed $115B corporate separation as a buy-side and sell-side advisory exercise. Modelled all three BD segments on a standalone DCF basis, evaluated four restructuring structures, and recommended a Reverse Morris Trust (RMT) with Thermo Fisher — directly relevant to VC investors assessing corporate carve-outs as investment targets or identifying sector-level disruption.

Key Assumptions
WACC — BD6.51% — after-tax cost of debt 2.61% (from BD annual reports), cost of equity 9.45% (CAPM: Rf 4.5%, beta 0.9, MRP 5.5%), D/C ratio 42.9%
WACC — Thermo Fisher7.05% — Kd 4.45% (interest expense / total debt), Ke 8.90%, D/C ratio 37.97%
Terminal growth rate2% for all segments — long-run GDP + inflation steady-state assumption
BD Life Sciences revenue growth5% per year (FY25–FY29) — conservative given FY24 actual of 1.1% and industry CAGR of 8.9%
BD Medical revenue growth6% per year — in line with MedTech industry CAGR of 6.3%
BD Interventional growth5% per year — below MedTech average, reflecting slower procedural recovery
Operating marginsLife Sciences 30.7%, Medical 31%, Interventional 28% — sourced from BD FY2024 Annual Report segment data
CapEx and depreciationCapEx at 4% of revenue; depreciation at 5% — MedTech and Life Sciences industry average
Working capital3% of revenue change each year — industry standard for diagnostics and MedTech firms
Tax rate21% — standard US corporate tax rate
Deal structure assumption40% cash ($12–14B) and 60% stock swap — preserves Thermo Fisher credit metrics and aligns BD shareholders with post-merger upside
Sector Context — Market Benchmarks Used
MetricBD (FY2024)MedTech IndustryLife Sciences Industry
Revenue growth (YoY)4.2%CAGR 6.3%CAGR 8.9%
BD Life Sciences revenue$5.2Bn/aBenchmark: sector leaders
Free cash flow growth+47.4%n/an/a
EPS growth (YoY)+16.3%n/an/a
Key Findings
BD Life Sciences valued at $27.9B standalone (24% of BD's total pre-separation EV of $115B); Thermo Fisher total EV at $156.9B — large enough to absorb the acquisition with moderate debt
RMT structure recommended over pure cash sale: BD avoids $5B+ in capital gains tax; deal structured at 40% cash / 60% stock balances BD shareholder liquidity with Thermo Fisher credit preservation
$500M–$1B in annual cost synergies identified across supply chain, R&D consolidation, and SG&A; plus revenue synergies from cross-selling BD diagnostics through Thermo Fisher's lab distribution network
Life Sciences underperformed its CAGR benchmark in FY24 (1.1% actual vs 8.9% industry) — validating the strategic rationale for separation and Thermo Fisher's premium offer
BD EV: $115B
LS Standalone: $27.9B
TF EV: $156.9B
Tax Avoided: $5B+
Synergies: $500M–1B/yr
FinTech Development and Bank Risk-Taking in India — MSc Dissertation
Research · EM FinTech Intelligence
Individual Dissertation · Cranfield University · Supervisor: Dr. Nemanja Radic · September 2025

Quantitative research study examining how India's FinTech revolution affects banking sector stability — directly relevant to VC investors sourcing deals in India's $5 trillion digital finance ecosystem and assessing disruption risk in B2B fintech portfolio companies.

Key Assumptions and Methodology
SampleBalanced panel of 33 Indian commercial banks — 13 public sector, 14 private sector — observed from 2010 to 2024 (495 bank-year observations)
Risk measureZ-score (ROA + Equity/Assets ÷ σ(ROA)) — the standard bank stability proxy in academic finance; higher Z-score means lower insolvency risk
FinTech Index constructionComposite index built from 20 Google Trends keywords across 5 dimensions: Payments, Lending, Investments, Identity, and Technology; annual averages of monthly search scores, normalised and equally weighted
Estimation strategyFixed Effects panel regression with bank-clustered robust standard errors; Hausman test confirmed FE over Random Effects (chi-sq 30.56, p < 0.001)
Lag structureFinTech Index entered with a one-year lag to reflect delayed impact on bank behaviour and reduce simultaneity bias
Bank controls includedSize (ln total assets), leverage (equity/assets), liquidity (liquid assets/total assets), efficiency (cost/income ratio), profitability (ROA)
Macro controls includedReal GDP growth, CPI inflation, RBI repo rate — sourced from World Bank WDI and RBI Database on Indian Economy
Exclusion criteriaCooperative banks and investment banks excluded — different regulatory regimes and business models would confound the commercial bank relationship being studied
Key Findings
Initial FinTech expansion increases bank risk-taking (β = −0.042, p < 0.01 in base FE model) — competitive disruption compresses margins and pushes banks toward riskier behaviour in the short term
Mature FinTech integration ultimately stabilises banks (β = +0.013 with robust SE, p = 0.013) — an inverted-U relationship that mirrors the adoption J-curve seen in other technology disruption contexts
Composite FinTech Index grew from 7.25 (2010) to 52.29 (2024) — proprietary sector data that quantifies the pace and acceleration of India's digital finance transformation
Heterogeneity finding: smaller, more efficient, income-diversified banks adapt faster — a pattern directly analogous to startup competitive dynamics that informs VC sector positioning in B2B fintech
Period: 2010–2024
Banks: 33
Observations: 495
Index range: 7.25 → 52.29
Adj. R²: 62.2%
